speaker: interviewer
text: September 1, 1995. Survivor Magda Vass. This is tape number two. Ms. Vass, you were telling me how the women were examining you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the rest of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very unpleasant, and then when they were through, they just let us mingle there with each other. And the following morning-- in fact, in some kind of trucks-- they took us close to the railway station, where there was a huge building for horses. Because in my hometown, and the county in itself, had a big business in exporting-- and maybe importing too-- horses. As a matter of fact, there was a family in our hometown that became very, very rich people. They all died. That's what they were doing, exporting horses. And I think they were importing from Italy and Spain and exporting to I don't know where. And that's why those big stalls were-- big buildings for horses-- horses' stalls-- were built, and that's where we  were put, in the horses' stalls. And we were there maybe a day or two. That's when my niece's 19th birthday was celebrated, in the horse stalls. Not celebrated, but was a very sad occasion. And that's where we were put in the wagons and taken to Auschwitz, because we didn't know where we were going. But I want to go back to my one experience in the ghetto, where my father died. One day, a man came, and he said, well, I want to go in the house and check out the apart-- the house where we lived-- because my wife wants to know what is the arrangement. And my father got very angry and said, you are not going into my house. There is no way I'll let you go in. Oh, we will have it anyhow, Mr. Vass. There is no way-- you go away, and we get the bakery, and we got everything. Well, my-- my father appeared at home [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before his second child was born. He was-- well, he-- he just was very upset and very angry, and ordered him out of the house. And he went. But he-- these are the people who wouldn't let me in when I went in, and called the genedarmes for me, that I am digging for treasures. Anyhow, my father got kind of sick. He really wasn't sick. He was in his 80s. He was a strong, healthy man. But he said-- when the-- the word came around that sooner or later, we will be transported from the ghetto-- he said, I am not ever leaving the house. They will have to take me. And one night, he just fell into a coma, and he died. But before that, when we knew he was sick, I don't remember how, but we let word go to my youngest brother, who was in the labor camp not too far from Kaposvar. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a labor camp there. And he came before my father died, a couple of days. The way he-- he escaped from the labor camp-- because he wasn't allowed to leave-- he went on a train, spent all that time in the toilet, and he got into the ghetto jumping the fence, and there he was. And we had a doctor with us also who was living in my parents' house, and he said, oh, if just Mr. Vago could have a little milk. So my brother, Imre, whom I adored, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] risking his life again, jumped the fence during the night and went to a family, gentile family, who lived not too far from us-- very well-to-do people-- and begged for a little milk for my father. My mother happened to friendly with the wife of that family. And they wouldn't give it to him-- to him, because they knew if he would be caught and tortured, who gave the milk, they would get into trouble. So he-- he came back empty-handed. And it was such a tragedy, because he really risked his life, but he-- it was in the middle of the night, dark. So he jumped the fence. He came back. And he went back the same way, on the train, in the toilet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I don't know, how we let him know again that my-- my father died. That the only way-- the only time they let us out of the ghetto to go to the cemetery. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were two Christian women kneeling at the empty grave there. One was a laundress who did my mother's laundry. Came to the house and did the laundry, I don't know, maybe 30 years, 40 years. I don't know. She was just-- she was just like a member of the family. That's how she made her living. And there was another one who lived across the street, but because it became the ghetto, she had to go out. And she was a very poor woman, and my father many times gave her bread-- don't pay for it. You know. And she was there. Those were the two people. And there was-- the rabbi died some time ago, but it was like an assistant rabbi who was allowed to be there. But we pushed it, pushed it, pushed it, because we hoped that Imre would be back again. And my mother insisted that a son has to give the Kaddish at the funeral. And we waited, all of us, and I see he's running into the cemetery. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he gave the Kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we buried my father there. And before he left, he came to me and to my niece. And he told us that he heard some horrible stories of Jewish women sent to the front and raped and killed. And whatever happens, we should avoid transportation wherever we will be, because that's what they do with young women. My niece was 19, and I was about 27. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then-- and then he left, running and hiding and got back to camp. That's the last time, of course, I've seen him. And when they talk about something hurts you, like a knife in your heart, that's true. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because after I went back to Hungary in 1945, and I heard-- I knew what happened to my mother. I knew that in Auschwitz-- but then I heard about my brothers, and I was thinking about Imre, it was just like a knife in my heart. I had a sharp pain in my heart. I really did, for a long, long time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After I got married, my husband-- of course, my second husband-- sent me to a psychiatrist, because I used to wake up in the night screaming for a long time. And that was really-- I think, you know, I was worried about my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did she suffer? Can you think about that? How long did she suffer in the gas chamber? How much pain did she have? How long did it take until she hopefully died? This-- this was tormenting me. It really was. So they say time takes care of everything. Not everything, but it heals wounds, you know. But that was my-- my terrible feeling for me, because I really-- I adored her, and she was like a saint.

speaker: interviewee
text: That woman never hurt a fly in her life. She was just a perfect mother, a perfect wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adored my niece, who was the first granddaughter. Everything was just so sweet about her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was-- when you were sent on a transport to Auschwitz, you were with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in the same wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law, my mother, my sister, my niece, my sister's mother-in-law, and the sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers? None of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all in labor camps. But at that time, we didn't know where they were, except that one, who was not too far from our hometown. Yeah, I guess we didn't know any more where they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in Auschwitz in a wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: The wagon-- I'm sure you know about the wagons, which we were-- we were-- we were sitting there like one on top of the other. We had a big pail to eliminate. And every once in a while, they opened the wagon door a little and dumped what was in it. It was horrible in the wagon. Some people died in the wagon. It was hot-- no air in the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: A wagon train, like, yeah, for transporting animals. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then all of a sudden-- [INAUDIBLE] it took at least three, four days until we got to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the train stopped-- there was a little-- very narrow little slit that we could look out-- and I see the name Auschwitz. And I said, Auschwitz. And an old Jewish man said, you don't know German. That's not 'ausch,' that must be Auswitz. I said, no, it is Auschwitz. It's spelled with 'sch' and not s-- you know, 'sh' or whatever. Auswitz would be 'ausw.' So we had a little argument. I had a little argument with that old man. Anyhow, it was Auschwitz. We didn't know where we were. No idea. Never heard of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then the wagon stops. And all these uniformed guards, who were actually prisoners-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in these blue and white striped outfits, which we got too-- and they were just ordering us to get off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were, you know, the wagons had three high steps, two or three high steps. And some just didn't do it. They just threw them down like a piece of object. Before you knew it, there was a pile of bodies there. They were just throwing-- throwing them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were also some-- there was my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother, in perfect German, says to that guy, Bitte schon hilfen mir, which means, please help me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I almost died there. But he picked her up and helped her down the steps. That was my mother. Even there, she was such a lady. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bitte schon hilfen mir. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After then, of course, I went down easily, and my niece and my sister, and we start to walk. And there were some guards, and they said, you go this way, you go that way. Left or right. And they separated me from my mother. And they asked my sister how old is she. I can't remember my German. And she was thinking for a minute, and she said 38, which wasn't true. She was 40. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe that helped her. Anyhow, they separated us, and I went with-- to my mother. And they started to holler at me, but I just kept going with her. And my daughter said no, absolutely not. You go with your sister and [INAUDIBLE]. I go with [NON-ENGLISH], that was my sister's mother-in-law. Let me just go with them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I went with my sister. Of course they were-- all-- those all were put in the gas chamber right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were ordered to take everything off, and just put everything in a-- whatever you have in one pile, because when you get out of the showers, you will find everything. [CHUCKLES] They were so full of lies. So we went into the shower, which came just-- very little water coming slowly, but it was wonderful. Because-- we just opened our mouths and had the water, because we didn't have water for all that time in the wagon. We didn't have any water. We had some food which we brought with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we come out of the shower, of course with not a room. There was nothing that we had before. And we got these robes. Some we got, some didn't, because they ran short with it. Some just had blankets, those held blankets. And then they got the red cross on the back with the paint. That was a prisoner's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emblem, or whatever you want to call it. So you couldn't escape. Who would ever escape from Auschwitz? There was no way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And Auschwitz was a living hell. If there was ever hell on Earth, it was Auschwitz. We were put into a building which hardly had any roof, or if it had some, it leaked all over when it was raining. But we didn't mind, because we  were terribly, terribly thirsty. So the rain was a blessing. We went just like that. We were there-- bodies one on the top of the other, because there were at least three times as many people-- women, only women in that Birkenau lager. Auschwitz-Birkenau. We were in Birkenau. At least three times as many as could go in. We weren't-- Oh, I didn't-- forgot to say that when we got out of the showers, we were all shaved completely. Not only completely-- became completely bald, but even our female-- our female organs. Everything-- not one piece of hair was left on us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So while we were there, sometimes I'm, where is my sister? I thought that was a young man lying there. You didn't recognize each other because of the baldness. We all looked like men somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, we were lying there, one on the top of the other, the buttock on the other buttock. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a horrible thing there. Then say our prayers, you know, but saying our prayers hard. All the time in the scorching heat there in Auschwitz-- scorching heat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what time of year and what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken there. My sister's-- my niece's birthday was July 3rd. We were going away maybe on the 5th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we have the zahlappells, and we've got blisters on our-- on our skulls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe these appels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me? Zahlappells is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe them.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's counting the bodies. You know, how many are there. The Germans are very good at keeping things in order. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the time we got there, they didn't care about our names. We didn't get tattoos, because it was already late for them. Actually, Roosevelt could have saved all Hungarian Jews by that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They needed workers, and that's why we were shipped out of Auschwitz after three, three and a half weeks, sometime in August. But meanwhile, we were dying from thirst. We were dying from hunger. We got a horrible-looking soup which wasn't soup, just gray-ish water looking like bark of a tree in it. And it's-- it's strange, but we all had diarrhea. We had no food, but we had diarrhea. There were big latrines, and we were standing in line for the latrines. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a little pool left over from some rain, and we almost drank from it, but it looked yellow from urine, so we didn't. Then my niece and I, we had a very-- very serious conversation how to commit suicide, because it was impossible to live through that. I'm sure we wouldn't have another week. Hunger, thirst, heat-- scorching heat. We were all full of blisters from the heat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if you just moved the wrong way, the Aufseherinnen, which are the supervisors with the whip, ran and hit you wherever they could reach you with the whip. And dogs all over-- barking dogs, threatening dogs. It was hell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a barbed wire-- first we thought we can hang ourselves, but we had nothing to hang ourselves with. We looked. We couldn't find any rope or anything. We were very serious about it. Then we thought, maybe we can do something with the electric wire, but what can we do? We have nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: A wagon came by some days with water, but if you didn't happen to find a cup, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you didn't have water. And you had to run like crazy, and before you knew it, the wagon was empty. It came by so late. Sometimes we found a cup, and they-- somebody came and hit us and took the cup out of our hands. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It-- it is impossible to describe Auschwitz. Impossible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So another person came-- Dr. Mengele. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to take off everything, and we had to stand in line, all completely naked. He came-- he was a sight, in a perfect uniform, with a-- with a whip and white gloves.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forget the white gloves. What does he need the white gloves for? It was a hot day. I didn't know who he was, but I remember his face. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he selected-- that was the selection, again. Describe his face, and describe him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dark eyes. Threatening eyes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dark hair. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. I-- Not a big mouth. It's hard to describe it. Dark skin-- he wasn't very white. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But he had something in the eyes which was cruel. Or not cruel maybe, but strict. He knew what he was doing, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was the boss, that kind of face. And he selected us-- one went left, one went right, one went the third place. And you knew-- did you know at this time what these selections meant? You knew what--

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that we were selected for something. We didn't know exactly. I knew, when I arrived in Auschwitz, I could-- I have a very sensitive sense of smell. And I right away smell burning flesh-- flesh, but I didn't know if it was chicken or whatever. I just could smell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a day or two later, I knew what it was. I even told my niece about it. I-- but they-- they killed my mother. And they burned what then they do with all the dead bodies but burn them? My niece writes about in the book where I said that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, that point in time, I didn't know what will happen. We were-- most of us were younger than those who were put in the gas chamber. My sister was maybe one of the oldest. So my sister was sent to the left, and I was sent to the right, and my niece was sent to the right, which means we are separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my niece starts to scream. I go after my mother, and they scream at her, you want to be shot? You go where you were told to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she just ran like crazy, and I-- I am not going to be separated. So I started screaming, she is my mother too! She's my mother too! Which of course wasn't quite right. And I am running, and I could feel a-- a-- a shot in my back, but I was screaming. I was running as fast as I could after my niece, and there was a throng of women around, and we escaped. I don't know if they shot or not, but they shooting-- shot at us. Or they-- they-- I don't remember-- I don't remember hearing shots, so maybe they didn't target them. They'll die anyhow. You know, that attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we met my sister there, and she was kneeling down, and she held us tight, and she said-- she said, this is the happiest day of my life that you are with me. The happiest day in Auschwitz. But it was very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we survived that day, because those who were sent, we turned-- nobody came back on that side. They were all dead. The young ones like us-- we had some from my hometown that nobody came back. Later on we heard they were sent some-- some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] copper mines, if I remember well. But nobody came back. We were sent to Hessich Lichtenau in Germany to work in a munition factory. The factory wasn't really in Lichtenau. It was in [NON-ENGLISH]. We went-- we walked maybe an hour to a train, then we were on the train for a certain period of time. I don't remember. And then we walked from the train to an underground factory. It was all underground. You couldn't see a thing. It was like a forest. And then you went down into the bottom, or the core of the earth, where that huge factory was.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it consisted of two different things. One was-- which was my job-- to live the shell. You know what a shell is, which comes out of armor or guns? It was very heavy-- maybe 25, 30 pounds. I had to lift it and put it in a-- in a vise, lift it up. It was a drill press, a huge machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the top of that machine came down on that shell. You know, I had to put that shell into a certain-- a certain way to be held. And that top of the machine came down and engraved some numbers into that shell. And then I had to take it out and put it on a dolly.
